sections.header: Verses 1-6 — "From the Depths of Sheol I Called"

heb.gloss: Sheol / the grave / the realm of the dead
heb.paragraph: Jonah equates his experience inside the fish with descent into the underworld. Sheol in Hebrew thought is not hell but the place of the dead, the furthest point from God's presence. The word creates a theological paradox: Jonah fled 'from the presence of the LORD' and ended up in Sheol — yet even there, God heard him.

The same verb used in God's commission to Jonah ('call out against Nineveh,' 1:2). The prophet who refused to 'call' to Nineveh now 'calls' to God from the belly of the fish. The wordplay links disobedience and prayer.

refs.note: David's prayer from distress uses nearly identical imagery: cords of death, torrents of destruction, depths of Sheol — followed by God hearing from his temple.
'Deep calls to deep in the roar of your waterfalls; all your waves and breakers have swept over me' — language Jonah echoes in his prayer.
Calling on God's name from the depths and receiving the assurance 'Do not close your ears to my cry' — the same pattern of supplication Jonah follows.

notes.note: Jonah prayed from inside the fish, demonstrating that no location is beyond God's reach. The belly of a fish at the bottom of the sea is as accessible to prayer as the temple in Jerusalem.
The phrase 'from the depths of Sheol I called' indicates Jonah understood he was at the very threshold of death. God's rescue was not incidental but purposeful.
Jonah's cry 'I have been banished from your sight' reflects the consequence of his own flight. He chose to flee from God's presence (1:3), and now experiences the existential terror of God's absence.

Calvin observes that Jonah's prayer is itself a miracle: the prophet prayed from a place where prayer seemed impossible. This teaches that no extremity of circumstance can separate the believer from access to God. The fish's belly became a chapel.

Calvin notes the tension between 'I have been banished from your sight' and 'yet I will look again toward your holy temple.' Faith and despair coexist in the same breath — a pattern familiar from the Psalms and from Christian experience.

The parallelism 'In my distress I called to the LORD / from the belly of Sheol I cried' equates the interior of the fish with the realm of death. The structure is synonymous parallelism: the second line intensifies and specifies the first.

The MT reads 'I have been driven away from your sight' (nigrashti). Some MSS and versions read the first person differently, but the sense is the same: Jonah experiences exile from God's presence as the consequence of his own flight.

Stuart argues that the psalm is not a later interpolation but an integral part of the narrative. Its thanksgiving genre (rather than lament) indicates that Jonah already regards his rescue from drowning as accomplished — the fish is deliverance, not punishment. The language is heavily indebted to the Psalter, which Stuart views as evidence of Jonah's priestly-prophetic training.

Stuart traces the descent imagery to its nadir: the waters surround him, the deep engulfs him, seaweed wraps his head, he sinks to the roots of the mountains. This is the lowest point in the book's vertical geography: heaven → land → sea → deep → Sheol. From this ultimate depth, Jonah cries upward to God's temple.

hist.context: Jonah's prayer is a thanksgiving psalm, not a lament — a surprising genre for a man still inside a fish. The prayer draws heavily on the Psalter, weaving together phrases and images from multiple psalms (Pss 18, 42, 69, 88, 120, 142). This has led some scholars to view it as a later insertion, but the canonical reading treats it as an integral part of the narrative: Jonah praises God for deliverance already experienced (rescue from drowning) while still awaiting full deliverance (release from the fish). The language of descent continues: waters, deep, roots of mountains, the pit.

heb.paragraph: The climactic declaration 'Salvation comes from the LORD' (yeshuʿatah laYHWH) is the theological center of the book. The noun shares its root with the name Joshua/Jesus. Jonah confesses a truth that will be tested when God extends this same salvation to Nineveh.

Literally 'breaths of emptiness.' Jonah contrasts idol-worshippers who forfeit God's covenant love (hesed) with himself, who will fulfill his vows. The irony is sharp: in chapter 1, the pagan sailors demonstrated more authentic worship than Jonah.

refs.note: 'From the LORD comes deliverance' — the psalmic source of Jonah's climactic confession, using the same Hebrew phrase.
The NT parallel: salvation by grace, not works. Jonah's declaration prefigures the Pauline doctrine that salvation originates entirely in divine initiative.

notes.note: Those who cling to worthless idols forfeit the grace that could be theirs. This is a general theological principle that Jonah applies to pagans, unaware that he himself is the one forfeiting grace by refusing to extend it.
'Salvation comes from the LORD' is the theological thesis of the entire book. If salvation is God's prerogative, then God has the right to extend it to whomever he chooses — including Nineveh.
The fish vomited Jonah onto dry land at God's command. Even the fish obeys God's word, in contrast to the prophet who initially refused.

Calvin sees here the classic Reformed distinction: salvation is entirely of God's free grace, not human merit. Jonah's confession is doctrinally correct, but the book will reveal that the prophet has not yet grasped its full implications — that divine mercy extends beyond Israel.

The phrase 'worthless idols' (havle shav) echoes Deut 32:21 and Jer 2:5, where Israel itself is accused of chasing 'worthless things.' The allusion sharpens the irony: Jonah accuses pagans of idolatry while he himself disobeys God.
The declaration yeshuʿatah laYHWH ('Salvation belongs to the LORD') is a nominal sentence with the force of an absolute theological claim. The word order places 'salvation' first for emphasis.

Stuart identifies v. 9 as the thematic keystone of the entire book. The declaration that salvation is the LORD's prerogative establishes the principle that Jonah will resist in chapters 3-4. Stuart notes that the psalm moves from distress to thanksgiving to confession, following the standard pattern of the todah (thanksgiving offering psalm) genre. Jonah's vow to sacrifice and fulfill his vows anticipates his physical return to the land and to prophetic service.

Stuart observes that the fish functions as a vehicle of both preservation and transportation. The vomiting onto dry land is not degrading but salvific: it returns Jonah to terra firma and to the possibility of obedience. The scene marks the structural midpoint between commission (1:1-2) and recommission (3:1-2).

hist.context: The psalm concludes with a pivotal theological declaration: 'Salvation comes from the LORD.' This is simultaneously a confession of personal gratitude and the central claim the rest of the book will develop. If salvation is from the LORD, then the LORD determines its recipients — including Ninevites. Jonah speaks the truth that will become the grounds for his own anger in chapter 4. The vomiting onto dry land (v. 10) echoes creation imagery: the sea releases onto land, reversing the descent pattern and beginning Jonah's restoration.

rows.label: vv.1-6
rows.text: Descent into the Deep: Distress and Cry

rows.label: vv.7-10
rows.text: Ascent from Sheol: Thanksgiving and Salvation

lit.note: The psalm exhibits a classic descent-ascent structure. Verses 1-6 trace Jonah's plunge to the nadir of Sheol; verses 7-10 recount God's rescue and Jonah's thanksgiving. The pivot is v. 7: 'my life was ebbing away, I remembered the LORD.'

themes.note: Mercy and Worship both peak at 8: the psalm is a thanksgiving for divine rescue. Faith at 7 as Jonah trusts God from the belly of the fish. Sovereignty at 7 as God commands the fish and governs Sheol itself.

ppl.name: Jonah
ppl.text: Jonah's prayer is a thanksgiving psalm, not a lament — a surprising genre for a man still inside a fish…

trans.title: Comparing translations in Jonah's Prayer from the Fish

translations.text: But I, with shouts of grateful praise, will sacrifice to you. What I have vowed I will make good. I will say, ‘Salvation comes from the Lord.’ ”
But I with the voice of thanksgiving will sacrifice to you; what I have vowed I will pay. Salvation belongs to the LORD!”
He said: “In my distress I called to the Lord, and he answered me. From deep in the realm of the dead I called for help, and you listened to my cry.
saying, “I called out to the LORD, out of my distress, and he answered me; out of the belly of Sheol I cried, and you heard my voice.
